description: Affective political polarization worldwide, encoded from WTW (Willis Towers Watson) Political Risk Index H1 2025, section 'Mapping political polarization around the world'. Figure 3 global time series (1900-2024) included. Narrative findings are tagged 'measured value'; the Figure 3 series is a manual chart digitization and therefore tagged 'assumed value' with per-point confidence.

words.name: affective polarization
words.description: Degree to which people perceive supporters of other political parties as hostile / view them with dislike.

words.name: elite polarization
words.description: Degree to which political leaders are unwilling to accept each other as legitimate.

words.name: ideological polarization
words.description: Degree to which people in a country are divided on fundamental political issues.

words.name: global average
words.description: Unweighted/aggregate average across the covered countries and territories.

words.name: political polarization
words.description: Division of a society or its political elite into mutually antagonistic groups.

words.name: world
words.description: All countries and territories covered (more than 200).

words.name: polarization index value
words.description: V-Dem-derived expert-rated affective polarization score; scale shown 1.8-2.6 in Figure 3.

words.name: displeased if child married across party lines
words.description: Survey question: would be displeased if their child married someone from the opposing political party.

words.name: measured value
words.description: Provenance qualifier: value taken from a stated source.

words.name: assumed value
words.description: Provenance qualifier: value estimated/digitized, not directly published as a number.

words.name: Figure 3
words.description: WTW PRI H1 2025 chart: Global average affective polarization, 1900-2024.

time_series.name: Global average affective polarization (Figure 3)
time_series.provenance_note_for_user: Values manually digitized by reading the Figure 3 curve against its gridlines (axis 1.8-2.6, gridlines every 0.2). They are NOT published numbers. Confidence is per-point; turning points (1915 trough, 1944 spike, 1971 peak, 2003 trough, 2024 top) are more reliable than mid-segment interpolations. Replace with exact V-Dem v2cl_acjst / polarization series values when available.

claims.statement: Affective polarization has been rising rapidly worldwide in recent years on a global-average basis.
On the V-Dem-based measure, global affective polarization has shot up since around 2000 to levels unprecedented in modern history.
Surges in polarization in democracies tend to be accompanied by the rise of populist movements and increased political violence.
Truth-and-reconciliation processes (Uruguay, Timor-Leste, Chile) and international war (post-WWII Norway, Luxembourg, Denmark; Ukraine) have historically reduced polarization.
The WTW measure relies on expert assessments (V-Dem, Fragile States Index) rather than opinion polls, which the report itself notes is probably less accurate than poll-based measures.
